claimText: Ways and Means Advances (WMA) are temporary advances used to cover receipt-payment mismatches and are not meant to finance fiscal deficits.
evidenceExcerpt: Ways and Means Advances (WMA) are temporary advances by RBI to central and state governments to cover receipt-payment mismatches, not to finance fiscal deficits.
examUse: Statement-based question on the primary purpose and limitations of WMA.
distractorHooks: WMA is a tool used by the government to finance its fiscal deficit.
WMA is a long-term borrowing mechanism for infrastructure projects.

claimText: Ways and Means Advances (WMA) are provided by the RBI to both the Central government and state governments or Union Territories.
evidenceExcerpt: Available to both the Central government and state governments/Union Territories.
examUse: Statement-based question identifying the eligible entities for WMA.
distractorHooks: WMA is exclusively available to the Central government.
WMA is provided by the RBI to commercial banks facing liquidity crises.

claimText: The interest rate charged on Ways and Means Advances (WMA) is the RBI repo rate, while overdrafts are charged at 2 percent above the repo rate.
evidenceExcerpt: Interest rate on WMA is the RBI repo rate, and overdrafts are 2 percent above the repo rate.
examUse: Statement-based or correct-pair question on the cost of borrowing under WMA.
distractorHooks: The interest rate on WMA is linked to the Bank Rate.
Overdrafts under WMA are charged at the Marginal Standing Facility (MSF) rate.
WMA is an interest-free advance provided to the government.

claimText: Ways and Means Advances (WMA) must be fully paid off within a maximum period of 3 months.
evidenceExcerpt: WMAs must be fully paid off within 3 months.
examUse: Statement-based question testing the tenure of WMA.
distractorHooks: WMA must be paid off within 14 days.
WMA has a repayment period of up to 1 year.
WMA can be rolled over indefinitely.

claimText: Ways and Means Advances (WMA) are statutorily authorized by the RBI Act 1934.
examUse: Statement-based question on the statutory backing of WMA.
distractorHooks: WMA is authorized under the Fiscal Responsibility and Budget Management (FRBM) Act, 2003.
WMA is authorized under the Banking Regulation Act, 1949.
